original_abstract:
This review examines a specialized class of molecules featuring bare group-14 atoms (C–Pb) that are stabilized through donor–acceptor interactions. Unlike traditional tetrylenes, which involve electron-sharing bonds, these "tetrylones" (carbones, silylones, etc.) possess a unique electronic structure where the central atom retains two lone pairs and acts as a bidentate Lewis acid toward σ-donor ligands. Using modern quantum chemical methods, including charge- and energy decomposition analyses, we characterize the bonding in these divalent E(0) species and demonstrate how their distinct electronic profiles lead to chemical reactivities that differ sharply from their carbene and silylene counterparts. 

Furthermore, we explore the bonding in transition metal complexes [TM]–E containing terminal, naked tetrele atoms. Our analysis reveals that these atoms bind to the metal center through a sophisticated combination of σ and π electron-sharing bonds alongside π-backdonation. These terminal ligands exhibit a high degree of "hidden" reactivity, making them excellent candidates for further adduct formation with Lewis acids—a theoretical prediction that suggests a roadmap for the future synthesis of currently unknown main-group and transition-metal species.
humanized_abstract:
Here we focus on a class of molecules that have bare group-14 (C–Pb) cores stabilized through donor–acceptor bonds. These are known as tetrylones (carbones, silylones, germanylones, etc.). These molecules constitute a completely new class of donors due to the fact that bare divalent group-14 atoms have two lone pairs on the central atom and behave as bidentate Lewis acids toward σ-donors. Using state of the art quantum chemical tools such as charge and energy decomposition analysis, we explore the nature of the bonding in these interesting species, and explain their chemical behavior in comparison to their carbene, silylene analogs, and shed light on their peculiar properties.

In addition we elucidate the bonding situation in transition metal complexes [TM]–E containing terminal, naked tetrel atoms (E = Ar, Si, Ge). Based on detailed analysis we conclude that these naked species interact with the transition metal center by means of an interesting σ- and π-bonding as well as significant π-backdonation and show a pronounced amount of “hidden” reactivity, suggesting large potential towards further adduct formation of Lewis acids thus suggesting synthesis strategies for unknown main-group and transition metal adducts.
